steps.title: Contrast old and new views
steps.content: The passage first explains that the cerebellum has long been recognized for coordinating motor control in humans. That focus made it harder for scientists to recognize other possible functions of the structure. The passage then shifts to recent neuroscience from the last two decades, which suggests that the cerebellum also helps regulate emotion and social behavior. Marino and colleagues' finding of a pathway to the VTA, a center for motivation and reward processing, adds support for these broader roles.

steps.title: Reject narrowed summaries
steps.content: Choice A incorrectly treats the VTA pathway as confirmation of the cerebellum's motor role, but the VTA is linked to motivation and reward processing. Choice B invents widely accepted claims about the pathway's function; the text presents the pathway as a recent finding, not as a disputed old claim. Choice D blames technological limitations and new technologies, but the passage says recognition was hindered by the focus on motor control. Choice C captures both parts of the main idea: the traditional motor view and the newer evidence for additional functions. The answer is C.
